attributes.stem:
A 35-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a four-day history of gradually worsening left knee pain. She works as a cleaner and spends significant time kneeling. She denies any history of trauma. She is able to walk but with difficulty due to the pain. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 90/min, blood pressure 110/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. The anterior aspect of her left knee is swollen but not tender to touch. There is no joint line tenderness and she has full range of motion of the knee joint.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial investigation?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with clinical features consistent with prepatellar bursitis, likely related to occupational kneeling. The key examination findings are anterior knee swelling without significant tenderness or limitation of joint movement. The diagnosis is essentially a clinical one, but depending on the severity of the patient's symptoms and the clinical findings, so some investigations might be considered.

Ultrasound is the most appropriate initial investigation as it can readily demonstrate fluid within the prepatellar bursa and assess for any surrounding soft tissue changes. It is non-invasive, cost-effective, and provides sufficient information for diagnosis.

Magnetic resonance imaging would be excessive for initial investigation of prepatellar bursitis. While it can provide detailed imaging of all knee structures, it is typically reserved for cases where there is diagnostic uncertainty or concern for complications such as osteomyelitis.

Plain radiograph would be appropriate if there was concern for fracture or dislocation based on history of trauma, which is not present in this case. X-rays do not visualise soft tissue swelling well enough to diagnose bursitis.

Joint aspiration would be indicated if there was concern for septic bursitis, typically presenting with fever, marked tenderness, and erythema. This patient's presentation is more consistent with non-septic bursitis.

Arthroscopy is an invasive procedure that would not be appropriate for investigating prepatellar bursitis, as the pathology is external to the knee joint.
learningPoints: Prepatellar bursitis commonly occurs due to occupational kneeling and presents with anterior knee swelling without restricted range of motion
Ultrasound examination is the most appropriate initial investigation for suspected prepatellar bursitis
Septic bursitis typically presents with fever, warmth, and marked tenderness which helps differentiate it from non-septic causes
references: Baumbach SF, Lobo CM, Badyine I, et al. Prepatellar and olecranon bursitis: literature review and development of a treatment algorithm. Arch Orthop Trauma Surg 2014; 134:359-70.
Khodaee M, 2017, Common Superficial Bursitis, Am Fam Physician. 2017; 95:224-231.

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
